coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of silver nanoparticles using carbon dots as reducing and stabilizing agents and their antibacterial mechanisms
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Jian-Cheng
Xu, Zi-Qiang
Dong, Ping
Lai, Lu
Lan, Jia-Yi
Jiang, Feng-Lei
Liu, Yi
coredata.dc:description: Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) are potent and broad-spectrum antimicrobial agents. Herein, a novel one-step method has been used to synthesize AgNPs, in which fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were used as reducing and stabilizing agents. To the best of our knowledge, the use of CDs as reducing and stabilizing agents has rarely been reported in conjunction with their use in the fabrication of AgNPs. Subsequently, their antibacterial activities were investigated. The results demonstrated that the surface property of AgNPs could influence the stability of AgNPs leading to different bactericidal effects. The size of the synthesized AgNPs was smaller when CDs were doped with sulfur. These small AgNPs exhibit better stability in culture medium contributing to excellent bactericidal activity, which can completely inhibit the growth of Escherichia coli (E. coli) at a concentration of 150μM of silver atom. Finally, the possible antibacterial mechanisms of AgNPs were proposed as follows: first, AgNPs can be absorbed easily onto the surface of E. coli and affect the permeability and fluidity of the outer membrane; second, AgNPs with smaller size (7.3±1.0 and 6.1±0.8nm) can permeate the membrane of E. coli to interact with DNA and the respiratory chain; and third, the release of Ag+ can cause E. coli death.

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detecting the concentration of silver atom by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy
The stability of AgNPs in culture medium
The integrity of the outer membrane of E. coli cells
The fluidity of the E. coli cell membrane
Generation of ROS detected by flow cytometry
Stability of AgNPs under different pH values
Stability of AgNPs in culture medium
Effects of AgNPs on membrane properties of E. coli

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM photographs of the four types of synthesized AgNPs: (A) AgNPs1, (B) AgNPs2, (C) AgNPs3, and (D) AgNPs4.
Growth thermogenic curves of E. coli treated with CDs: (A) CDs1, (B) CDs2, (C) CDs3, and (D) CDs4. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Growth thermogenic curves of E. coli treated with AgNPs: (A) AgNPs1, (B) AgNPs2, (C) AgNPs3, and (D) AgNPs4. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Relationship between k, I, and the concentrations of AgNPs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The stability of four types of AgNPs in the culture medium: (A) AgNPs1, (B) AgNPs2, (C) AgNPs3, and (D) AgNPs4. The black line denotes AgNPs suspensions in water. The other colors represent AgNPs suspensions in the culture medium from t =0–2, 4, 6, 8, 16, 24, 32, 48, and 56min. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The outer membrane permeability of E. coli treated with or without 100μM AgNPs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The membrane fluidity of E. coli treated with or without 100μM AgNPs: (A) probed with DPH and (B) probed with HP. The error bars represent standard error of the mean. ** P <0.01 versus the control group. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
TEM images and ultrathin section of E. coli cells. The concentration of AgNPs was 100μM.

The ROS level in E. coli detected by a flow cytometer. The concentration of AgNPs was 100μM. The error bars represent standard error of the mean. ** P <0.01 versus the control group. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Schematic of AgNPs synthesis and the mechanisms for their antibacterial activity: (a) AgNPs with smaller size (7.3±1.0 and 6.1±0.8nm) penetrate damaged E. coli cells and then interact with DNA in the cytoplasm and the respiratory chain on the cytoplasm membrane. (b) Release of Ag+ from AgNPs absorbed on the surface of E. coli. (c) Release of Ag+ from AgNPs that penetrate damaged E. coli. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Parameters of E. coli growth at different concentrations of AgNPs1.
Parameters of E. coli growth at different concentrations of AgNPs3.
Parameters of E. coli growth at different concentrations of AgNPs4.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of silver nanoparticles using carbon dots as reducing and stabilizing agents and their antibacterial mechanisms

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Virology & Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Virology & Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education)
College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Yangtze University, Jingzhou 434023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) are potent and broad-spectrum antimicrobial agents. Herein, a novel one-step method has been used to synthesize AgNPs, in which fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were used as reducing and stabilizing agents. To the best of our knowledge, the use of CDs as reducing and stabilizing agents has rarely been reported in conjunction with their use in the fabrication of AgNPs. Subsequently, their antibacterial activities were investigated. The results demonstrated that the surface property of AgNPs could influence the stability of AgNPs leading to different bactericidal effects. The size of the synthesized AgNPs was smaller when CDs were doped with sulfur. These small AgNPs exhibit better stability in culture medium contributing to excellent bactericidal activity, which can completely inhibit the growth of Escherichia coli (E. coli) at a concentration of 150μM of silver atom. Finally, the possible antibacterial mechanisms of AgNPs were proposed as follows: first, AgNPs can be absorbed easily onto the surface of E. coli and affect the permeability and fluidity of the outer membrane; second, AgNPs with smaller size (7.3±1.0 and 6.1±0.8nm) can permeate the membrane of E. coli to interact with DNA and the respiratory chain; and third, the release of Ag+ can cause E. coli death.

ce:section.ce:para: It is widely known that silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) can be used as electrodes [1–3], catalysts [4,5], and surface-enhanced Raman-scattering (SERS) materials [6,7], because of their excellent chemical and physical properties. In addition, as a new generation of nanoproducts with biomedical applications, AgNPs also attract considerable attention especially for their excellent antibacterial activities [8,9]. Recently, AgNPs have also been found to accelerate wound and burn healing [10]. However, the mechanisms of their antibacterial activities are still not fully understood. Lok et al. implied that the antibacterial mode of action of AgNPs is similar to that of Ag+ through proteomic analysis [11]. Kittler et al. studied the kinetic release of Ag+ from AgNPs during storage, and they showed that the toxicity of AgNPs is a result of the slow dissolution of AgNPs [12]. Liu et al. also observed the same phenomenon [13]. Xiu et al. suggested that the toxicity of AgNPs is not related to a specific particle property but it is caused by the oxidation of silver by oxygen and subsequent Ag+ release [14]. At the same time, they showed that reactive oxygen species (ROS) are not an important antibacterial mechanism for silver ions, and ligands can hinder the toxicity of Ag+ significantly while they affect the toxicity of AgNPs minimally [15]. Katherine et al. investigated the interaction between Ag+ and the respiratory chain of Escherichia coli, and they demonstrated that AgNPs can uncouple the respiratory chain from adenosine triphosphate (ATP) synthesis [16]. Jung et al. observed changes in the cell membrane after treatment with Ag+, which they speculated to be the main reason for cell death [17]. Li et al. also found that AgNPs can damage the cell membrane [18]. Morones et al. found that AgNPs in the range of 1–10nm can permeate E. coli [19]. Further, previous study has proposed that AgNPs could increase the ROS level [20], inhibit the activity of respiratory enzymes [21], damage DNA, and inhibit DNA duplication [22]. Nevertheless, whether it is a single or synergistic effect could not be confirmed.

The shape [23], size [24,25], surface modification [26], surface charge [27], and especially the stability of AgNPs are crucial to their antimicrobial properties. Therefore, many synthesis routes were designed to improve the antibacterial properties of AgNPs. In many cases, AgNPs were synthesized with high energy consumption or sodium borohydride, which could not easily control the reduction process because of their very powerful reducing ability. However, they can be easily aggregated when either no or weak stabilizers are used, thus further reducing their antibacterial properties. To overcome this limitation, some novel stabilizing and reducing agents are urgently required.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), a new carbon material, can be used in bioimaging [28,29], biosensing [30], drug delivery [31], photocatalysis [32,33], etc. Recently, it has been reported that the fluorescence of graphene quantum dots can be enhanced by hydrazine hydrate reduction [34]. Furthermore, the reduced CDs have been used to prepare gold nanoparticles with an incubation period of 24h [35]. Moreover, the maximum emission wavelengths of CDs are reversible when treated with sodium borohydride and nitric acid [36]. Therefore, one can conclude that CDs have both reducing and oxidizing abilities. Furthermore, numerous –COOH and –OH existing on the surface of CDs can coordinate with Ag+ and stabilize AgNPs. Considering this, four types of CDs were used to rapidly synthesize AgNPs in a one-step method. Then, we examined the antibacterial activities of the synthesized AgNPs against E. coli through microcalorimetry, and the results showed that two types exhibit excellent antibacterial activities, which can completely inhibit the growth of E. coli at a concentration of 150μM of silver atom. Finally, the antibacterial mechanisms were studied, and important factors were proposed.

Sucrose, acetylcholine chloride, mercaptosuccinic acid, N-acetyl-l-cysteine, oleic acid, ether, etc. were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). Silver nitrate, peptone, 1,6-diphenyl-1,3,5-hexatriene (DPH), and 1,3,5,8-tetramethyl-2,4-bis(alpha-hydroxyethyl) prophine-6,7-dipropionicacid (HP) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2MΩcm (Millipore) was used in all synthesis methods.

CDs were synthesized according to the method reported by Li [37]. In particular, impure CDs were obtained by heating 5g of sucrose and 10mL of oleic acid in a three-neck flask at a temperature of 215°C for 5min while vigorously stirring. The other three types of CDs were obtained by heating 4g of sucrose and 3g of acetylcholine chloride, 4g of sucrose and 3g of mercaptosuccinic acid, and 4g of sucrose and 3g of N-acetyl-l-cysteine at their corresponding melting points for 15, 6, and 15min, respectively. After cooling, the supernatant liquid was discarded and the solid product was obtained at the bottom of the flask. The precipitate was dispersed with water and then extracted with ether several times in order to remove the remaining oleic acid. Finally, the products were freeze-dried and obtained after being dialyzed in deionized water using a 1-kDa membrane for 1day.

First, 100mL of water was boiled to 100°C, and then the obtained CDs (125μL, 50mg/mL) were added and boiled for 15min while stirring. Finally, AgNO3 (5mL, 3.42mg/mL) was added subsequently after NH3·H2O (1mL, 10%) was injected. The reaction was processed for 45min and cooled to room temperature. Finally, ultrafiltration was conducted with a 10-kDa membrane in order to remove the remaining silver nitrate, and the AgNPs were obtained.

The absorption spectra of the obtained AgNPs were tested on a MAPADA double-beam spectrophotometer at room temperature. The morphology of the CDs and AgNPs was observed with a JEOL 2011 transmission electron microscope (TEM) at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potentials of the AgNPs were measured using Nano-ZS ZEN3600 (Malvern Instruments, Malvern, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out with a KRATOS XSAM800 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer using Mg as the exciting source. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the CDs were recorded by a Thermo (USA) FTIR spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting the concentration of silver atom by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: An Intrepid XSP Rasial (Thermo, USA) with a concentric model nebulizer and a cinnabar model spray chamber was used to determine the concentration of silver atom in the four types of synthesized AgNPs. In an ampulla, 2μL of purified AgNPs was digested by nitric acid to almost dryness while being heated. Finally, 2mL of deionized water was used to wash the ampulla, and the solution was prepared for the experiment of inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES).

E. coli (DH 5α) was incubated with sterile Luria-Bertani (LB) medium consisting of 10g of NaCl, 10g of peptone, and 5g of yeast extract per liter at a pH value of 7.2 and temperature of 37°C. A colony of E. coli grown on LB agar plates was inoculated in 30mL of fresh medium and grown in a shaking incubator at 37°C for 12h. Then, 600μL of the new bacterial suspension was added to 30mL of fresh medium and grown at 37°C for 10h. The bacteria were harvested by centrifugation at 5000g for 5min and washed twice with sterile phosphate-buffered solution (PBS) (pH=7.4) and resuspended in PBS to an optical density (OD) at 600nm of 0.4. The bacterial concentrations were determined by measuring OD at 600nm (OD of 0.1 corresponds to a concentration of 108 cells per cm3). When the membrane property was investigated, the generation of ROS in E. coli cells was detected, morphology of the E. coli cells was observed, and the resuspended E. coli cells were used.

An LKB-2277 isothermal microcalorimeter (Thermometric AB, Jarfalla, Sweden) was used to record the heat flow of the E. coli cells during their growth and metabolism. All calorimetric experiments were conducted at a temperature of 37.0°C. The stopped-flow method was used in this study. In all of the experiments, the flow cell was completely cleaned and sterilized in sequence by pumping 25mL of aqueous HCl (0.1M), 25mL of aqueous NaOH (0.1M), and 25mL of sterilized distilled water assisted by an LKB-2132 MicroPerpex peristaltic pump. When a stable baseline was obtained, the cell suspension containing the bacteria and different concentrations of CDs and AgNPs were pumped into the flow cell. When the flow cell (volume, 0.6mL) was filled, the pump was stopped, and the thermal effect versus time associated with microbial activities was recorded by a computer. The remaining bacterial suspension was discarded. Generally, the experiments were stopped when the second peak came to an end. All experimental procedures were performed under aseptic conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability of AgNPs in culture medium
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the synthesized AgNPs in culture medium was determined by the absorption spectra of AgNPs. The control was culture medium, and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were obtained every 2min. A new absorption peak indicated the aggregation of the AgNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The integrity of the outer membrane of E. coli cells
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of different types of AgNPs on the integrity of the outer membrane were studied by examining their sensitization to detergent-mediated bacteriolysis. Bacterial cells were treated with or without AgNPs at a concentration of 100μM for 2h at a temperature of 37°C. Then, the mixtures were centrifuged, and the supernatant was removed. The cell pellets were resuspended in the same volume of sterile PBS buffer without AgNPs. Finally, sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) (0.1% final concentration) was added, and OD600 was continuously monitored.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluidity of the E. coli cell membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial cells were treated with or without AgNPs at a concentration of 100μM for 2h at a temperature of 37°C. To 3mL of each cell suspension, 0.6μL of the fluorescent membrane probe, DPH (20mM stock solution in tetrahydrofuran), was added and incubated for 60min in the dark at room temperature to allow the probe to incorporate into the cytoplasmic membrane. The alteration of E. coli membrane fluidity was investigated by an LS-55 spectrofluorometer (Perkin-Elmer Corporation, USA). The excitation and emission wavelengths were 354 and 427nm, respectively. When probed with HP (3μL of 6mM stock solution in ethanol), the incubation time was 5min. The excitation and emission wavelengths were 520 and 626nm, respectively.

Electron microscopic observation was carried out using a TECNAI G2 TEM at an accelerating voltage of 100kV. After completing the exposure period, cells (control and exposed group) were washed with PBS buffer. For TEM examination, 1-μL aliquot of the treated and untreated E. coli cells was placed on a carbon-coated copper grid. In addition, all ultrathin sections for the TEM were prepared following standard procedures for fixing and embedding of biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generation of ROS detected by flow cytometry
ce:section.ce:para: After completing the pretreatment of AgNPs, the cells (control and exposed group) were washed with PBS buffer. The obtained cells were incubated with 10μM 2,7-dichlorodihydrofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA) for 30min in the dark. Then, the mixtures were centrifuged and the supernatant was removed. Subsequently, the E. coli cells were resuspended in PBS for the flow cytometry experiments.

Prior to obtaining four types of AgNPs, the CDs were synthesized. The four types of CDs were fabricated according to the method reported by Li [37], using sucrose, sucrose and acetylcholine chloride, sucrose and mercaptosuccinic acid, and sucrose and N-acetyl-l-cysteine as the starting materials. The average size of as-prepared CDs was 2.4±0.9, 2.4±1.0, 2.6±0.3, and 2.4±0.8nm, respectively (Fig. S1).

The functional groups of the four types of CDs were identified by FTIR spectroscopy. The broad peak centered at 3388–3421cm−1 represents the stretching vibrations of O–H. The existence of a sharp absorption peak at 1630–1665cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibrations of CO (Fig. S2A, B, D). Another sharp peak at 1720–1736cm−1 associated with COOH stretching vibrations is also observed (Fig. S2B–D). In addition, the peaks at 1245–1265 and 1023–1040cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of C–O–C, respectively. Several peaks at 2927–2930 and 1400–1414cm−1 reveal the presence of CH2 and CH3 on the CDs. Moreover, peaks at 1586, 1556, 1400, and 1228cm−1 can be assigned to asymmetric stretching vibrations of COO−, bending vibration of N–H, bending vibration of C–H and C–N, and stretching vibrations of Ar–O–C (Fig. S2C, B, D).

We also used XPS to investigate the element states of the CDs, and the results were shown in Fig. S3. All the four types of CDs show two apparent peaks centered at 284.8 and 532.3eV, which can be attributed to C 1s and O 1s. The peak at 399.8eV associated with the binding energy of N1s is observed for CDs2, indicating that CDs2 is N doped (Fig. S3B). In addition, CDs3 and CDs4 show two peaks at 229.0 and 161.3eV that belong to S2s and S2p (Fig. S3C and D). In addition, N1s is also observed for CDs4 (Fig. S3D). On combining this information, we can conclude that CDs2, CDs3, and CDs4 are N, S, and N–S doped, respectively. The results are in good agreement with FTIR.

In order to understand the process of the reaction, the temporal evolution of the UV–vis spectra was recorded. A representative temporal evolution of the UV–vis spectra for the synthesis of AgNPs1 is demonstrated in Fig. S4A. Within 1min of addition of AgNO3 to the boiling CDs1, a pronounced color change occurred in suspension from colorless to light yellow and finally brown-yellow. The inset is the corresponding evolution of absorbance intensity at 402nm versus time; it is likely that the reaction could be divided into two stages. At stage (I) of the reaction (5–20min), the growth of AgNPs1 and a significant increase in the absorbance intensity were observed. At stage (II) of the reaction (30–60min), the absorbance intensity decreased a little, then increased slowly, and finally became inclined to remain stable together with a slight blue shift of the absorption peak, which is in perfect agreement with the process of Ostwald ripening. Fig. S4B showed the hydrodynamic diameter of AgNPs1 characterized by dynamic light scattering (DLS). At first, the hydrodynamic diameter decreased continuously at 5–20min, which may be a result of the combined effects of CDs1 consumption and AgNPs1 generation. Because the surface of the water-soluble CDs consists of large amounts of hydrophilic groups such as –OH and –COOH, these hydrophilic groups can be hydrated considerably, resulting in the large hydrodynamic diameter of CDs. Then, the size gradually remained stable, consistent with the result of UV–vis spectroscopy. The as-synthesized four types of AgNPs show the characteristic surface plasmon resonance (SPR) peak at about 402, 409, 410, and 404nm, respectively (Fig. S6), thus demonstrating that the four types of AgNPs were successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of AgNPs under different pH values
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the as-synthesized AgNPs1 under different pH values was studied by UV–vis spectroscopy and DLS. In the experiments, the pH values were adjusted using diluted NaOH and HCl solution. As shown in Fig. S5A, when the pH value was under 3, the absorbance intensity at 402nm was perceptibly lower than that of the pH value between 3 and 12. The same phenomenon was observed when a pH value of 13 was achieved. Almost no difference is presented between the pH value of 4 and 12 with respect to the absorbance intensity at 402nm. This indicates that the synthesized AgNPs show excellent stability property. The results were proven by DLS, as shown in Fig. S5B. The diameters were nine times larger when the pH values were <3 or >12 than when the pH values ranged from 3 to 12; this might be due to the aggregation of AgNPs. Interestingly, there is no clear difference in the size of the AgNPs1 from the pH value of 4–12 compared with the results of pH values of 1, 2 and 13, which confirmed the results of the UV–vis spectroscopy perfectly.

Fig. 1 shows the TEM photographs of the four types of AgNPs synthesized by four different types of CDs. It can be seen that well-dispersed AgNPs were obtained. The corresponding size distribution of the obtained AgNPs were fitted well with the Gaussian distribution (Fig. S7), and the average size was 23.4±2.8, 17.2±2.3, 7.3±1.0, and 6.1±0.8nm, respectively.

The surface functional groups and element states of AgNPs were characterized by XPS. The four types of AgNPs show two apparent peaks centered at 284.8 and 532.3eV, which can be attributed to C 1s and O 1s, respectively (Fig. S8). Another peak at 161.1eV associated with S 2p was observed in the XPS spectrum of AgNPs4. In addition, the typical Ag 3d XPS spectrum shows the binding energy of Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 at 368.1 and 374.1eV, respectively (Fig. S9), which are consistent with Ag (0) [38]. Fig. S10 shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s for four types of AgNPs. The entire spectrum can be fitted into four types of carbon bond, corresponding to sp2 (CC) at 284.5–284.6, sp3 (C–C) at 285.4–285.6, C–OH at 286.3–286.7, as well as CO at 287.4–287.9eV [28]. However, C–S may also exist in AgNPs3 and AgNPs4 as the binding energy of both C–S and C–C is near 285.5eV [39,40]. It should be noted that XPS only reflects the surface components of AgNPs; the relative contents of the core component cannot be obtained.

The as-synthesized four types of AgNPs were used to study the antibacterial activity against E. coli using a biological microcalorimeter. Microcalorimetry is a useful tool to study microorganism metabolism as it can easily record the specific growth power–time curves of a microorganism because it is universally present, integral, nondestructive, and highly sensitive, as proved by our group [41–43]. In this experiment, an LKB-2277 Bioactivity Monitor was used to study the effects of AgNPs on the growth metabolism of E. coli. The concentration of the synthesized AgNPs was defined as silver atom concentration determined by ICP-AES. Before investigating the antibacterial activity of AgNPs, we studied the effects of four types of CDs on the growth thermogenic curves of E. coli first. The results are shown in Fig. 2 . We can see that different concentrations of CDs (0–200μg/mL) do not show any toxic effect on the growth of E. coli. Fig. 3 shows the thermogenic curves of E. coli growth in the presence of different concentrations of the four types of AgNPs. As shown in Fig. 3A, controlling the concentrations of AgNPs1 below 100μM has little effect on the growth of E. coli. The log phase was delayed with increase in concentration. A significant lag of the log phase and decrease in the maximum heat production were observed while the concentration of AgNPs1 reached 200μM. However, the half inhibition level could not even reach a concentration of 200μM when treated with AgNPs2 (Fig. 3B). By contrast, AgNPs3 and AgNPs4 exhibited excellent antibacterial activity, and the growth of E. coli was completely inhibited when the concentration of AgNPs reached 150μM (Fig. 3C and D).

The increase of heat power (P) is exponential during the log phase. The growth thermogenic curves of the log phase correspond to Eq. (1):
(1)
ln
P
t
= ln
P
0
+ kt
where t is the incubation time, Pt is the power output at time t, P 0 is the power output at time t =0, and k is the growth rate constant. By using linear regression of lnPt versus t, the growth rate constant k of the microorganism can be obtained. The inhibitory ratio I can directly evaluate the antibacterial activity of AgNPs, which can be calculated through Eq. (2):
(2)
I = (
k
0
-
k
c
) /
k
0
where k 0 and kc are the growth rate constants for non-treated and AgNP-treated E. coli. Therefore, the bactericidal effect of AgNPs on the multiplying metabolism of E. coli can be analyzed through the changes in k and I. In addition, the maximum heat power P m and the heat output Q are also important parameters for the metabolism of a microorganism as they reflect the ability of the microorganism to grow under in vitro environment conditions. By analyzing the growth thermogenic curves of E. coli in Fig. 3, the thermokinetic and thermodynamic parameters for the metabolism of E. coli treated with AgNPs with good antibacterial activity were obtained (Tables 1–3
).

P m decreases with an increase in the AgNPs concentration in a dose-dependent manner. The heat production Q of E. coli decreased when the AgNPs1 concentration was low, but it was higher than control when the AgNPs1 concentration was beyond 150μM. However, it was minimally affected by AgNPs3 and AgNPs4 (Fig. S11). The relationship between the growth rate constant k, suppress ratio I, and concentration c is shown in Fig. 4 . The growth rate constant k clearly decreases with the increase in AgNPs concentration, suggesting that AgNPs can inhibit the metabolism of E. coli well. The k–c curves demonstrate an exponential relationship for AgNPs1, whereas they show an obvious linear relationship for AgNPs3 and AgNPs4. Using Eq. (2), the half inhibitory concentration (IC50) can be calculated. The value of IC50 can reflect the inhibition capability of a compound quantitatively. The smaller the value of IC50, the stronger is its inhibitory activity. Therefore, the antibacterial activity of the four types of AgNPs can be compared based on their IC50 values. The IC50 values of E. coli for AgNPs1, AgNPs3, and AgNPs4 were 194.42, 81.35, and 83.72μM, which indicate that AgNPs3 and AgNPs4 exhibit a better bactericidal activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of AgNPs in culture medium
ce:section.ce:para: For the mechanism investigation, it is necessary to consider the stability of the four types of AgNPs in culture medium because it could seriously influence the antibacterial activity. The results are shown in Fig. 5 . On the one hand, the absorbance intensity of AgNPs1 and AgNPs2 at about 400nm decreases with the passage of time. On the other hand, a new absorption peak appeared at 560 and 620nm for AgNPs1 and AgNPs2, respectively. A dramatically red shift occurred gradually from 560 to 650nm and from 620 to 680nm during the monitoring period for 54min. This may be due to the aggregation of AgNPs1 and AgNPs2 in the culture medium because of salt and nutrients [44,45]. However, AgNPs3 and AgNPs4 did not show a new absorption peak, indicating that they did not aggregate, which could be further proved by obtaining a TEM photograph of E. coli. Furthermore, the relationship between the new absorption peak and time is a decaying exponential relationship (Fig. S12). Herein, we assume that the extent of AgNPs aggregation can be reflected by the absorption peak, with the aggregation intensifying until it reaches an equilibrium. At first, the AgNPs concentration was high and the aggregation speed was fast. Later, the active AgNPs concentration that can be aggregated became low, and the aggregation rate decreased. The surface charge of nanoparticles has a great influence on their stability. To identify the stability differences of the four types of AgNPs, their zeta potentials were measured. The results showed that AgNPs3 and AgNPs4 have a value of zeta potential lower than −30mV, indicating a good stability of AgNPs3 and AgNPs4, whereas AgNPs1 and AgNPs2 demonstrated a zeta potential slightly higher than −30mV (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of AgNPs on membrane properties of E. coli
ce:section.ce:para: The outer membrane of E. coli is isolated from cells, serving as a barrier to hydrophobic substances and macromolecules [46]. In addition, the transport of nutrients across the bacterial membrane depends on the permeability and fluidity of the outer membrane. Therefore, the bacterial membrane is vital to cell functions. When treated with AgNPs, it first interacts with the outer membrane of E. coli. Thus, it is necessary to study the effects of AgNPs on the membrane property of E. coli. The destabilization of the outer membrane leads to an increased susceptibility to the bacteriolytic action of amphiphilic molecules, which cannot penetrate an intact outer membrane [47]. In general, E. coli is almost impermeable to 0.1% SDS. However, 0.1% SDS can easily permeate E. coli if the outer membrane is damaged. Fig. 6 shows that pretreatment of E. coli with AgNPs sensitized cells to bacteriolysis by 0.1% SDS, as demonstrated by a decrease in the bacterial turbidity (OD600). When treated with AgNPs1, the permeability of E. coli dramatically increased. We reasoned that the increase was perhaps caused by the accumulation on some part of the outer membrane. AgNPs3 and AgNPs4 show nearly equal effects, but they are weaker than AgNPs1. On the other hand, AgNP2 exhibits the least effect on the outer membrane permeability of E. coli, possibly a result of the high-level aggregation.

To evaluate the membrane fluidity, two probes, DPH and HP, were used to label the membrane. DPH is associated with the lipophilic tails of phospholipids in cytoplasmic membranes with minimal membrane perturbations owing to its hydrophobic and symmetrical properties [48]. While HP mainly probed the hydrophilic area and acyl group of the membrane [49]. When excited with polarized light, they emit polarized fluorescence. The rotational diffusion of the fluorophore is related to fluorescence depolarization, and it can reflect the membrane fluidity. The higher the polarization value, the lower the membrane fluidity. With the treatment of AgNPs, the membrane fluidity decreased (Fig. 7 ). In detail, the fluidity of the hydrophobic area of the membrane decreased significantly when treated with AgNPs4. AgNPs1 showed more pronounced changes in the decrease of the membrane fluidity than AgNPs3. A significant decrease in the fluidity of the hydrophilic area of the membrane was observed when treated with AgNPs3. Both AgNPs4 and AgNPs1 did not demonstrate any obvious effect. Interestingly, AgNPs2 showed little effect on the fluidity of the E. coli membrane in either the hydrophobic or hydrophilic area, which may be due to the serious aggregation of AgNPs2.

TEM was used to observe the morphology of E. coli clearly when treated with AgNPs. Fig. 8 shows the TEM images of E. coli cells treated with AgNPs with good antibacterial activity. Unexposed to AgNPs, the surface of E. coli is smooth and clear. When treated with AgNPs1, the outer membrane was damaged. It may be due to the enrichment of AgNPs1 on some area of E. coli because of aggregation and ion release. On the other hand, AgNPs3 and AgNPs4 was just absorbed on the surface of E. coli with no obvious damage to the outer membrane and cell wall.

Morones has reported that AgNPs may penetrate E. coli sized 1–10nm [19]. Fortunately, the average size of AgNPs3 and AgNPs4 is smaller than 10nm. Considering this, ultrathin sections were made to confirm whether AgNPs can penetrate E. coli (Fig. 8). Without pretreatment of AgNPs, the integrity of E. coli cells is preserved and the surface is clean. With the treatment of AgNPs1, a few particles are absorbed on the surface of E. coli cells. However, when treated with AgNPs3 and AgNPs4, large amounts of the AgNPs absorbed onto the E. coli cells and parts of AgNPs penetrated the cells at the damaged areas of the membrane. The cellular degradation was also accompanied by electron-translucent cytoplasm and cellular disruption in the damaged cells.

Many reports illustrated that ROS is the key factor responsible for the death of cells when treated with AgNPs [50–52]. DCFH-DA is the most commonly used probe to detect the intracellular ROS level. In itself, DCFH-DA cannot emit fluorescence. Once it penetrates the cells, it can be hydrolyzed by enzymes in cells to produce DCFH. After oxidation with ROS, it can exhibit strong fluorescence. A flow cytometer was used to detect the cellular ROS level of E. coli. Surprisingly, only parts of DCFH-DA can penetrate E. coli cells, possibly because of the barrier of their cell wall. When treated with AgNPs, the uptake of DCFH-DA by E. coli increased especially for AgNPs1. The results of the generation of ROS are shown in Fig. 9 . AgNPs1 and AgNPs2 induced a higher level of ROS than AgNPs3 and AgNPs4, which may be due to their size difference. For the four types of AgNPs in all, the ROS level shows an increase of about <50%. In comparison with Carlson’s result of AgNPs inducing a >10-fold increase in ROS levels in macrophage cells [25], the result in this study indicated that ROS is not chiefly responsible for the death of E. coli cells.

CDs have been widely studied for their application in bioimaging, biosensing, photocatalysis, etc., but their reducibility and capping property have rarely been exploited. The four types of synthesized CDs were used as capping and reducing agents to fabricate well-dispersed AgNPs composites by a one-step method. The reduction process of Ag+ is closely related to pH values. Without ammonia, a minimal amount of AgNPs is produced. This may be attributed to the higher reducing activity of CDs under high pH values. We investigated the effects of time on the synthesis of AgNPs, and we found that well-dispersed AgNPs were obtained for about 45min (Fig. 1). The stability of the obtained AgNPs1 at different pH values was studied (Fig. S5). Almost no SPR peak was found when the pH value was lower than 2, which indicated that almost all AgNPs aggregated in solution. The obtained AgNPs exhibit good stability under a wide pH window as their minimal change from pH value of 3–12 is illustrated by UV–vis spectra and DLS. It is reported that AgNPs have strong antimicrobial effects as well as low toxicity, good durability, and no drug resistance [53]. However, the exact mechanism of action of AgNPs as an antibacterial agent is not yet fully understood. Many reports have explained that the antimicrobial activity of AgNPs against gram-negative bacteria depends on the concentration of AgNPs, which has been proven by our results (Fig. 3). The stability of the AgNPs is an important factor influencing the antibacterial activity [54]. The aggregation of AgNPs1 and AgNPs2 in the culture medium might be the main reason for their poor bactericidal performance. AgNPs2 is the most unstable of the four types of synthesized AgNPs, therefore exhibiting the worst antibacterial activity. AgNPs3 and AgNPs4 show good stability, therefore exhibited good bactericidal property (Fig. 4). The stability of AgNPs is closely related to their surface chemistry, which is related to the antibacterial performance. CDs3 and CDs4 are S and N–S co-doped, respectively. Sulfur has a high binding energy to silver. When CDs3 and CDs4 are used to synthesize AgNPs, the sulfur can bind tightly to silver. This may be attributed to the difference in stability for the four types of AgNPs. Therefore, it is necessary to use S-doped CDs to fabricate stable AgNPs in future. AgNPs3 and AgNPs4 have similar surface modification, resulted in their similar stability property in the culture medium. Besides, there is little difference in size between AgNPs3 and AgNPs4. Consequently, the IC50 values of E. coli cells for AgNPs3 and AgNPs4 are nearly the same.

When AgNPs and E. coli cells are mixed in a culture medium, the AgNPs first interact with the salts and protein, forming a protein corona [55]. Then, the protein-corona-capped AgNPs act on E. coli cells. The outer membrane acts a barrier for E. coli cells to prevent toxic substance from entering into E. coli cells. As excellent bactericidal agents, AgNPs can be defined as compounds toxic to E. coli cells. Therefore, it is essential to reveal changes in the membrane property of E. coli cells when treated with AgNPs. Previous reports have shown that the interaction between silver and the constituents of the bacterial membrane causes structural changes and damage to the membranes and intracellular metabolic activity, which might be the cause or consequence of cell death [23,56]. We found that AgNPs1 can significantly enhance the permeability of the outer membrane, which might be due to the enrichment of AgNPs1 as their aggregation was observed on parts of the E. coli membrane according to the TEM results (Fig. 8). However, a similar phenomenon did not occur as for AgNPs2 because of the very great extent of aggregation and different ion-releasing ability. The membrane fluidity of the hydrophilic and hydrophobic areas was significantly decreased when treated with AgNPs4 and AgNPs3, respectively, as large amounts of these nanoparticles were absorbed and accumulated on the surface of the E. coli membrane. Siva Kumar et al. proposed that oxygen associates with silver and reacts with the sulfhydryl (–SH) groups on the cell wall to form R–S–S–R bonds, thus blocking respiration and causing the death of cells [57]. The attachment of both silver ions and nanoparticles to the cell wall caused the accumulation of envelope protein precursors, which resulted in dissipation of the proton motive force. AgNPs also caused destabilization of the outer membrane and rupture of the plasma membrane, thereby resulting in depletion of intracellular ATP [11]. Numerous AgNPs3 and AgNPs4 are absorbed on the surface of E. coli cells (Fig. 8). They can increase the outer membrane permeability and they might easily bond to proteins containing sulfhydryl groups on the membrane, thus damaging the outer membrane. Sondi et al. reported that the bactericidal action of AgNPs was closely associated with the formation of “pits” in the cell wall of the bacteria. Then, AgNPs accumulated on the bacterial membrane caused an increase in permeability, resulting in cell death [56]. Unfortunately, these “pits” were not observed clearly in our experiment. Morones reported that AgNPs sized 1–10nm can penetrate E. coli and release Ag+, thus leading to cell death [19]. The size of AgNPs3 and AgNPs4 is smaller than 10nm, and ultrathin section experiments were conducted. Obviously, AgNPs1 cannot penetrate E. coli cells; only a few of them are absorbed onto the surface of E. coli cells (Fig. 8) because some of them aggregate in PBS buffer. AgNPs3 and AgNPs4 were found to be capable of permeating E. coli cells (Fig. 8), which may further act on the respiratory chain and result in the inhibition of metabolic activity of E. coli cells causing cell death. However, most of them were just on the surface of E. coli, and AgNPs3 and AgNPs4 were only found in damaged E. coli cells. Thus, we suppose that AgNPs3 and AgNPs4 can enter E. coli cells only if the cell wall is damaged. Meanwhile, Ag+ can be inevitably released from AgNPs when AgNPs are absorbed onto or enter E. coli cells, thus causing a series of events such as interaction with the respiratory chain and inhibition of cell division leading to E. coli death.

Release of Ag+ and generation of ROS are the two main mechanisms for the bactericidal activity of AgNPs against E. coli, as reported by previous studies [15,50,58]. In our experiments, the ROS level was enhanced by less than about 50% (Fig. 9), which showed minimal effect compared with other reports [25]. Therefore, ROS release is not an important event leading to E. coli death. Recently, Xiu et al. pointed out that Ag+ release from AgNPs contributes to their antimicrobial activity directly by demonstrating a lack of AgNPs toxicity when tested under strictly anaerobic conditions that preclude Ag atom oxidation and Ag+ release [14]; this further verified our results. For this evidence, we speculate that the absorption of AgNPs onto the surface of E. coli, their penetration of E. coli cells, and release of Ag+ are the main mechanisms contributing to their excellent antibacterial activity, and the schematic diagram is shown in Fig. 10 . Furthermore, only 150μM AgNPs (16.2ppm of Ag) can completely inhibit E. coli growth. This is superior to the results of Kang [53] (37.6ppm of Ag) and Song [23] (500ppm of Ag). Inspiringly, the as-synthesized AgNPs possess great stability for >10months, which satisfies the requirement of AgNPs as commercial antibacterial agents. Therefore, AgNPs fabricated using CDs in our study show vast potential for their use in antimicrobial systems.

Four well-dispersed types of AgNPs have been fabricated through a one-step reduction process using fluorescent CDs as reducing and stabilizing agents. The surface property can remarkably influence the stability of AgNPs resulting in different bactericidal properties. AgNPs synthesized by CDs containing sulfur have excellent stability, and they show enhanced antibacterial performance, which can completely inhibit the growth of E. coli at a concentration of 150μM of silver atom. We speculate that AgNPs act primarily in three ways against E. coli. First, AgNPs can attach to the surface of the outer cell membrane and disturb its proper function, such as membrane permeability and fluidity. Second, AgNPs with small size (7.3±1.0 and 6.1±0.8nm) are able to penetrate bacterial cells and cause damage by possibly interacting with intracellular substances such as DNA, protein, and the respiratory chain. Finally, release of Ag+ could cause E. coli death.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of China (21225313), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21473125), Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (2014CFA003), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2042014kf0287), and Large-scale Instrument and Equipment Sharing Foundation of Wuhan University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2015.05.084.
